revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉眼]]
[[File:Statues_of_Fūjin_and_Raijin_in_Sanjūsangen-dō._Mid-13th_century.jpg|alt=National Treasure [de; ja; draft]・Fūjin and Raijin [de; ja; draft] statue (Kamakura period, Sanjūsangen-dō)|thumb]]
'''Gyokugan''' (玉眼) is a technique used in Buddhist statues to render the eyes more lifelike by inlaying a plate of quartz ({{ill|Quartz|en|Quartz|qid=Q43010|lt=Crystal|1=Quartz}}). The earliest confirmed example dates to the first year of {{ill|Ninpei|en|Ninpei|qid=Q1193887|lt=Ninpei|1=Ninpei}} ({{ill|1151|en|1151|qid=Q19642|lt=1151|1=1151}}) and is found in the Amida triad at Nara’s {{ill|Chōgaku-ji|en|Chōgaku-ji|qid=Q11652171|lt=Chōgaku-ji|1=Chōgaku-ji}} ({{ill|Amida triad|de|Amida-Dreifaltigkeit|ja|阿弥陀三尊|lt=Amida triad statue|lt_ja=阿弥陀三尊像|lt_de=Amida-Dreifaltigkeitsstatue|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amida triad|1=Three Sages of the West|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10519939}}). This technique became widespread during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and was subsequently employed in many Buddhist statues.
== Overview == In sculptures created using {{ill|Inlaid wood construction|de|Furnier-Holzkonstruktion|ja|寄木造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inlaid wood construction|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏像|1=Buddha in art|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1000809}}, the head is also internally carved to form a hollow space. A hole is then made at the eye area into which a slightly larger, thin, polished crystal—shaped like a lens—is fitted from the inside and secured with wood dust. From the back of the crystal, the {{ill|Pupil|en|Pupil|qid=Q173318|lt=Pupil|1=Pupil}} as well as the outer corners ({{ill|Periorbital dark circles|en|Periorbital dark circles|qid=Q155888|lt=Eye corner|1=Periorbital dark circles}}) or even {{ill|Capillary|en|Capillary|qid=Q103142|lt=Capillary|1=Capillary}} are directly painted; then, {{ill|Fine cotton|de|Feine Baumwolle|ja|真綿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fine cotton|1=Silken Cotton|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11583482}} or {{ill|Paper|en|Paper|qid=Q11472|lt=Paper|1=Paper}} is applied to depict the white of the eyes. Finally, the assembly is pressed with a {{ill|Wooden piece|de|Holzstück|ja|木片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden piece|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and fixed in place using {{ill|Wood dust lacquer|de|Holzstaublack|ja|木屎漆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wood dust lacquer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屎}} and {{ill|Bamboo nail|de|Bambusnagel|ja|竹釘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo nail|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A prerequisite for creating Gyokugan is that the interior of the statue is hollow; the evolution of Japanese wooden Buddhist sculpture—with techniques such as {{ill|Internal carving|de|Innere Schnitzerei|ja|内刳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Internal carving|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Inlaid wood construction|de|Furnier-Holzkonstruktion|ja|寄木造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inlaid wood construction|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏像|1=Buddha in art|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1000809}}—provided the impetus for developing this method. In early Kamakura period inscriptions, alongside the name of the sculptor responsible for the entire work ({{ill|Busshi|en|Busshi|qid=Q2928765|lt=Buddhist sculptor|1=Busshi}}), the name of the Gyokugan craftsman is also sometimes recorded<ref>{{ill|Kaikei|en|Kaikei|qid=Q3191958|lt=Kaikei|1=Kaikei}}’s {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|qid=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} statue ({{ill|Daigo-ji|en|Daigo-ji|qid=Q1157535|lt=Daigo-ji|1=Daigo-ji}} Sanbō-in) and, similarly, the {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai statue|1=Vairocana}} ({{ill|Tokyo University of the Arts|de|Tokyo-Kunstuniversität|ja|東京藝術大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo University of the Arts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京芸術大学|1=Tokyo University of the Arts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1153515}}).</ref> This underscores how pivotal Gyokugan is in influencing a statue’s overall impression and indicates that Buddhist sculptors specialized in this technique due to the high level of craftsmanship required to process the crystal.
The technique of inlaying crystal into the eyes is also found in {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}}, and examples exist in {{ill|Greek sculpture|de|Griechische Skulptur|ja|ギリシャ美術|lt=Greek sculpture|lt_ja=ギリシャ彫刻|lt_de=Griechische Skulptur|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek sculpture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア美術|1=Greek art|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1247086}} where {{ill|Enamel|en|Enamel|qid=Q340324|lt=Enamel|1=Enamel}} is used, as well as in China where works incorporate gems, {{ill|Glass|en|Glass|qid=Q11469|lt=Glass|1=Glass}}, beads, or molded materials. In Japan, even during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, cast statues ({{ill|Statue|en|Statue|qid=Q179700|lt=Cast statue|1=Statue}}) exist in which the pupils were rendered with black stone (for example, the {{ill|Four Heavenly Kings|en|Four Heavenly Kings|qid=Q1068367|lt=Four Heavenly Kings|1=Four Heavenly Kings}} at the Kaito-in of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|qid=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}}), and in the statues of the {{ill|Twelve Heavenly Generals|en|Twelve Heavenly Generals|qid=Q842329|lt=Twelve Divine Generals|1=Twelve Heavenly Generals}} at {{ill|Shin-Yakushi-ji|en|Shin-Yakushi-ji|qid=Q3080305|lt=Shinyakushi-ji|1=Shin-Yakushi-ji}}, blown glass in green and brown was employed. Thus, although the inlaying of disparate materials into the eyes was mostly confined to wrathful images until the Nara period, after {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|qid=Q714061|lt=Ganjin|1=Jianzhen}} arrived in Japan during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the technique began to be used even in statues of Buddhas and bodhisattvas of the Danzo style<ref>{{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} (Nara, {{ill|Hokke-ji|en|Hokke-ji|qid=Q3076936|lt=Hokke-ji|1=Hokke-ji}}) with copper studs, or in the half-seated bodhisattva (Kyoto, {{ill|Gandoku-ji|de|Gandoku-ji|ja|願徳寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gandoku-ji|1=Gantoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665144}}) featuring black beads.</ref>. [[File:Bodhisattva_20241013_130825.jpg|alt=Standing Bodhisattva [de; ja; draft] (13th century, Important Cultural Property). A crystal is inlaid in the lips.|thumb]]
As a rare example, there are cases where only the eyes are inlaid with crystal<ref>{{ill|Kaikei|en|Kaikei|qid=Q3191958|lt=Kaikei|1=Kaikei}}’s {{ill|Fukū Gensaku Kannon|de|Fukū Gensaku Kannon|ja|不空羂索観音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukū Gensaku Kannon|1=Amoghapasha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11361835}} statue (at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|qid=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} Nan'en-dō) and the {{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|qid=Q357988|lt=Daikaku Zenji|1=Lanxi Daolong}} statue produced around the mid-{{ill|13th century|en|13th century|qid=Q7049|lt=13th century|1=13th century}} ({{ill|Kenchō-ji|en|Kenchō-ji|qid=Q1396078|lt=Kenchō-ji|1=Kenchō-ji}}) are known.<ref>Furthermore, although exceedingly rare, there exist Buddhist statues employing a technique—sometimes termed “Gyokuchin” (Crystal Lips)—in which crystal is also inlaid in the {{ill|Lip|en|Lip|qid=Q15173|lt=Lips|1=Lip}}. For instance, the "Standing Bodhisattva" (housed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, Important Cultural Property. [http://www.emuseum.jp/detail/100419/000/000?mode=simple&d_lang=ja&s_lang=ja&word=%E5%96%84%E5%86%86&class=&title=&c_e=&region=&era=&century=&cptype=&owner=&pos=1&num=1 Image and explanation available]) and the Habuki Amida statue (with a slightly open mouth revealing three or four teeth, at Kyoto’s {{ill|Busshō-ji|de|Busshō-ji|ja|仏性寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Busshō-ji|1=Busshō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60987047}}) illustrate this, suggesting that the Gyokuchin technique was employed in connection with the veneration of the {{ill|Living being|de|Lebendes Wesen|ja|生身|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living being|1=Shōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11574858}}.</ref>.
{{clear}}
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}
== References == * {{ill|Masumi Shimizu|de|Masumi Shimizu|ja|清水眞澄 (Buddhist Art Researcher)|lt=Masumi Shimizu|lt_ja=清水眞澄|lt_de=Masumi Shimizu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masumi Shimizu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ‘The Face of Buddhist Statues – Reading Form and Expression’ published by {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|qid=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}} (〈{{ill|Iwanami Shinsho|de|Iwanami Shinsho|ja|岩波新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Shinsho|1=Iwanami Shinsho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11266371}} (New Akahon) 1445〉), September 2013, pp.158-165, ISBN 978-4-00-431445-5
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Buddha in art|en|Buddha in art|qid=Q1000809|lt=Buddhist statue|1=Buddha in art}} * {{ill|Ocular prosthesis|en|Ocular prosthesis|qid=Q702379|lt=Prosthetic eye|1=Ocular prosthesis}}
{{translated page|ja|玉眼|version=104431386|comment=Imported full ja history}}
